topic: The Underground City That Cooked Itself
title: The Underground City That Cooked Itself
title_options: The Underground City That Cooked Itself
This City's Life Support Turned On It
How One Aquifer Killed a City of 9,400
The Machine That Farmed Its Own Killer
Sealed From the Outside, In Good Order
thumb_headline: Its own life support turned on it
thumb_prompt: a massive circular basalt hatch set into a cracked desert plateau at dusk, heat shimmer rising off the stone, a faint rust-black crust creeping from the hatch seams, one small human figure in dusty field gear standing before it, warm dim amber K-dwarf sunlight, low angle, ochre dust haze, real depth in the background, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain, bottom third of frame kept clear

thumb_options.concept: the sealed hatch with heat shimmer and creeping black crust, tiny human for scale
a single massive pipe descending into darkness, rust-black film blooming on its flank
a hand pressing a seal stamp onto hatch documentation, city lights dying below

settlement.name: Tehlmark Deep
settlement.premise: A city cut vertically into basalt to escape a surface that cooks — eleven levels, the deepest 340 metres down.

systems.name: COOLING
systems.how: A closed water loop gathers waste heat from coil banks on every level and rejects it into an aquifer 500 metres below the lowest gallery.

systems.name: WATER
systems.how: The drinking supply, drawn up, filtered, arriving pre-chilled by the deep rock.

systems.name: POWER
systems.how: Geothermal taps sunk into the same hot basalt the city hides from.

systems.name: AIR
systems.how: Passive stack ventilation — the day-night temperature swing at the surface pumps the shafts like a slow lung.

systems.name: FOOD
systems.how: Fungal galleries on level 9, fed on organic fractions sieved from surface dust.

hidden_dependency: Cooling and drinking water both terminate in one aquifer — a coupling nobody ever drew on the same diagram.

stress_event.name: The Bloom
stress_event.trigger: During dust season, a surface intake filter change went eleven days overdue — the replacement crew was repairing a crane.
cascade: Biologically active volcanic dust passes the tired filters and settles on the warm, wet cooling coils of every level
Cinder film colonises the coil surfaces and halves their thermal conductivity within 60 days
Pumps run harder to compensate; pump waste heat enters the same loop it is trying to empty
The aquifer, warmed by sixty years of rejected heat plus the new load, crosses 45 C — the film's optimal growth band — and growth accelerates
Cooling capacity falls below the geothermal baseline; deep-gallery air temperature rises roughly one degree per week
Drinking water reaches 46 C on day 211; evacuation ordered on day 214

verdict_reason: Two systems shared one aquifer, on a page nobody drew.
real_world_basis: Industrial biofouling of heat exchangers — a real, multi-billion-dollar engineering problem in cooling systems worldwide
Iron- and sulphur-metabolising extremophile biofilms (Acidithiobacillus, Sulfolobus; the Rio Tinto river ecosystem)
Thermal breakthrough in geothermal doublets — reinjected waste heat gradually warming the source aquifer
Closed-loop district cooling systems, where pump work itself becomes part of the heat load
Deferred-maintenance failure cascades, where a trivial scheduling slip compounds into system loss
premise: A buried city ran flawlessly for sixty-one years on five systems — two of which shared a resource nobody drew on the same diagram.
hero_prompt: the heat-exchanger gallery of an underground city — a long basalt vault lined with pipe manifolds and finned coil banks, warm amber worklights, thin haze, a single technician with a hand lamp for scale, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain, matte contrast
forbidden_visuals: a creature with a face or eyes
next_tease_topic: The Convoy That Outran the Sun
description: Original speculative documentary. The place is fictional; the principles are real. Tehlmark Deep buried itself to survive a desert that cooks — then its own life support, fed by a maintenance slip and a mineral-eating film, turned the city into an oven it could not switch off. Case one of the Kelvara survey files. #speculativefiction #worldbuilding #documentary

scenes.title: A city with nobody in it
scenes.narration: Three hundred and forty metres under the worst desert on this world, there is a city with nobody in it. The doors were sealed from the outside — carefully, in good order, by the people who left. Nothing broke in. Nothing exploded. The machine that kept nine and a half thousand people alive simply began, very slowly, to cook them. The air in its deep galleries held thirty-one degrees for sixty-one years. This is the story of what happened when it stopped. The place is fictional. The principles are real.
must_show: a sealed circular hatch in desert rock
heat shimmer
scenes.ai_prompt: a massive circular basalt hatch sealed into a cracked desert plateau, heat shimmer rising, fine ochre dust drifting past, dim warm amber late-afternoon sunlight, no people, documentary photograph, 35mm, slight film grain

scenes.title: The Cinder Shelf
scenes.narration: This is Kelvara. Fourth world of a small amber star. Its day runs thirty-one hours; its air is thin, and heat, once made, does not want to leave. And this is the Cinder Shelf — a basalt plateau where the afternoon lasts longer than a working shift, and the surface cooks anything that stands still on it. Nothing survives up here. Everything that lives on the Shelf lives underneath it. Thirty-one degrees, at depth, is not a comfort. It is a masterpiece.
must_show: the atlas locator on the Cinder Shelf

scenes.title: Tehlmark Deep
scenes.narration: Tehlmark Deep was cut in the survey's forty-first year. Eleven levels, straight down; the deepest gallery three hundred and forty metres below the hatch. Nine thousand four hundred people. Underground was not a hardship here — on a world with a weak magnetic shield, underground is simply where you live. Fungus galleries on level nine turned surface dust into food. Geothermal taps turned the rock's own heat into power. Thirty-one degrees, level nine, year after year.
must_show: a vast vertical shaft city interior
a human figure for scale
scenes.ai_prompt: interior of a vast vertical city shaft carved through black basalt, eleven ringed galleries descending into warm darkness, strings of amber worklights, railed walkways, small human figures on a gantry for scale, thin haze, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain

scenes.title: Five systems, one page
scenes.narration: Draw the city the way its engineers drew it. Five systems. Power: geothermal, tapped from the hot basalt below. Air: the long day itself pumps the shafts — warm air rising by day, sinking cool by night. Food: the fungal galleries. Cooling: a closed water loop that gathers heat from every level and rejects it into an aquifer five hundred metres beneath the lowest floor. And water: the drinking supply, drawn from that same aquifer, filtered, arriving pre-chilled. Five systems on one page. The diagram was correct. Every line on it was true. Thirty-one degrees.
must_show: the five-system dossier schematic
two lines terminating at one aquifer node

schematic.headline: Five systems, one page
schematic.body: Cooling rejects into the aquifer. The taps draw from it.
schematic.label: CORE TEMP

scenes.title: The best decision in the room
scenes.narration: It is tempting to call that shared aquifer a mistake. It was not. It was the best decision in the room. One aquifer meant one pump gallery, one maintenance crew, one set of spares, and drinking water that came up cold without spending a single watt to chill it. An audit in year fifty called the design elegant — the report survives. The engineers of Tehlmark Deep were not fools. Given the same data, you would have signed the same page. Thirty-one degrees, and the afternoon overhead could not touch them.
must_show: engineers at work in a pump gallery
massive pipe manifolds
scenes.ai_prompt: a deep pump gallery carved in basalt, two engineers in worn coveralls consulting a paper diagram beside a massive pipe manifold, warm amber worklight pools, condensation on dark stone, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain

scenes.title: Eleven days
scenes.narration: Year sixty-one. Dust season. The storms ran long, and the intake filters on the surface shafts — due for replacement every forty days — went eleven days over, because the replacement crew was repairing a crane. That is the entire trigger. No earthquake. No villain. A crane. Volcanic dust rode the night air down the shafts, through tired filters, and settled on the warm, wet coils of the cooling loop — on every level at once. And some of that dust was not mineral. By season's end, the loop's return line ran two degrees warm. Thirty-three.
must_show: dust pouring down a ventilation shaft
a clogged intake filter
scenes.ai_prompt: fine ochre dust cascading down a huge stone ventilation shaft in low amber light, a clogged industrial intake filter caked with dark grime in the foreground, haze thick in the air, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain

scenes.title: The film
scenes.narration: Under a hand lens, the culprit is almost nothing. A matte, rust-black crust, with a faint oily shimmer where it is growing. The field crews called it cinder film. It is not a plant, and it is not an animal. It is a colony that eats minerals — iron and sulphur, which is exactly what the Shelf's dust is made of — and it prospers between forty-five and seventy degrees. There is no malice in it. On an older world, engineers fight its cousins in cooling towers and call the problem biofouling. Here, it had just been handed the finest habitat on the planet. Thirty-four.
must_show: rust-black crust on a metal coil
faint iridescent bloom at its edge
scenes.ai_prompt: extreme close-up of a matte rust-black biofilm crust spreading across a finned metal cooling coil, a faint oily iridescent bloom along its growing edge, beads of condensation, warm amber worklight, macro documentary photograph, shallow depth of field, film grain

scenes.title: A blanket half a millimetre thick
scenes.narration: A heat exchanger works by letting heat cross a thin wall. The film is an insulator — a blanket half a millimetre thick, laid politely over every wall the city owned. In sixty days, the coils lost half their conductivity. The loop compensated the only way a loop can: pump more water, faster. But pumps are motors, and every motor is a heater. By spring, Tehlmark Deep was spending a fifth of its power just moving heat — and every joule of that spending became new heat to move. Thirty-seven.
must_show: conductivity falling on a dossier chart

scenes.title: Farming it
scenes.narration: Here is where the trap closes. The aquifer — the cold heart of everything — had been drinking the city's waste heat for sixty years, and it was no longer truly cold. Warm water grows film faster. Faster film means harder pumping. Harder pumping, warmer water. The engineers saw the whole shape of it within a week — and seeing changed nothing. Their power came from hot rock. The source could not be throttled, only wasted, and wasted heat went into the same water. The city was not fighting the film. The city was farming it. Forty-one.
must_show: the exchanger gallery degraded
black film advancing along pipes
scenes.ai_prompt: the heat-exchanger gallery of an underground city in decline — a long basalt vault of pipe manifolds and coil banks now streaked with rust-black film, haze thickening, one technician with a hand lamp standing very still, warm amber worklights dimmed by vapour, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain

scenes.title: Two moves left
scenes.narration: There were two moves left, and both were losing moves. Shut the loop down and starve the film — but this world's air is thin, and a sealed city does not cool. It soaks. A dead loop meant fifty degrees in the nurseries within two days. Or keep pumping, and feed it. They kept pumping. On day two hundred and eleven, the drinking water came up at forty-six degrees. On day two hundred and fourteen, the council ordered the walk-out: nine thousand four hundred people, eleven levels, up into the night. The last crew out sealed the doors. Forty-six.
must_show: a long line of people climbing a shaft stairway at night
belongings carried by hand
scenes.ai_prompt: a long orderly line of people in dusty field clothes climbing a vast spiral stairway cut into black basalt at night, carrying bags and children, lit by strings of amber emergency lights, haze below them, seen from above, documentary photograph, 35mm, film grain

scenes.title: Verdict
scenes.narration: Tehlmark Deep held for sixty-one years and fell in two hundred and fourteen days. Verdict: failed. Cause: two systems that shared one aquifer, on a page nobody drew. The city is still down there — intact, dark, its galleries at fifty-one degrees when the last sensor died. The Shelf keeps it sealed.
must_show: the verdict card
near-silence

card.headline: FAILED
card.body: Two systems shared one aquifer, on a page nobody drew.

scenes.title: The survey moves on
scenes.narration: The survey moved east after that, to the Shelf's edge — where the basalt walks down into a slow, warm sea whose tide arrives before its moon does. That file stays closed, for now. The next case is a city that refused to bury itself. It survives the long afternoon by never letting it arrive: a convoy, three kilometres long, that has been driving for ninety years. Next: The Convoy That Outran the Sun.
must_show: a distant coastline at dusk from high altitude
scenes.ai_prompt: aerial view at dusk of a dark basalt plateau descending in giant steps to a warm shallow sea, faint bioluminescent shimmer far out on the water, dim amber horizon light, ochre haze, documentary photograph from high altitude, 35mm, film grain
